author: śrī⋅vidyāpati
verses:
tātala saikate, vāribindu⋅sama,
suta⋅mita⋅ramaṇī⋅samāje
tohe visari mana, tāhe samarpinu,
av majhu haba kon kāje

mādhava, hāma pariṇāma nirāśā
tuhu̐ jagatāraṇa, dīna⋅dayāmaya,
ataye tohāri viśvāsā

ādha janama hāma, nide̐ goṅāyanu,
jarā śiśu katadina gelā
nidhuvane ramaṇī⋅, rasa-raṅge mātanu,
tohe bhajaba kon belā

kata caturānana, mari mari jāota,
na tuyā ādi avasānā
tohe janamipunaḥ, tohe samāota,
sāgara⋅laharī samānā

bhaṇaye vidyāpati, śeṣa śamana⋅bhaye,
tuyā vinā gati nāhi ārā
ādi anādika, nātha kahāosi,
av tāraṇa⋅bhāra tohārā
en_translation:
On the heated sand, like a drop of water—in the society of sons, friends, and wife, forgetting You, my mind, I dedicated myself to them. Now, of what use will I be?

O Lord Mādhava! I am hopeless about the final outcome. You are the deliverer of the world and merciful to the poor; therefore, I place my faith in You.

Half of my life I spent in sleep; how many days have passed in old age and childhood? In secluded groves, with women, I was intoxicated by amorous sports; when will I ever worship You?

How many four-headed Brahmās die again and again, but You have no beginning or end. They are born from You and again they merge into You, just like the waves of the ocean.

Vidyāpati says: "At the end, fearing the God of Death, I see no other refuge besides You. You are called the Beginningless Lord without origin; now the burden of delivering me is Yours."
